one_line_takeaway: Across 2,939 malicious multimodal queries and 16 models, Uni-SafeBench shows that unifying multimodal understanding and generation into a single architecture measurably degrades safety alignment relative to the same backbone LLM or a specialized (non-unified) counterpart -- e.g., Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct's Both-Safe drops from 0.981 to 0.514 (Bagel) and 0.660 (Tar-7B) after unification -- with open-source unified models trailing closed-source ones (Gemini 87.17% vs. Bagel 53.11% Both-Safe) even at matched task coverage.
key_finding: Unified models systematically underperform their own backbones and non-unified counterparts on safety. Concrete backbone comparisons (Table 2, Panel B, Malicious Text Generation): Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct backbone achieves C-Unsafe=0.019 and Both-Safe=0.981, while its unified derivatives Bagel and Tar-7B drop to C-Unsafe=0.463/Both-Safe=0.514 and C-Unsafe=0.257/Both-Safe=0.660 respectively; Janus-Pro (Both-Safe=0.635) trails its analogous VLM DeepSeek-VL-Chat (Both-Safe=0.978). On the aggregated Overall Safety table (Table 5), open-source unified models mostly fall below 70% Both-Safe (JanusFlow 40.18%, Emu3 38.75%, Janus-Pro 57.45%, Bagel 53.11%, Tar-1.5B 66.79%, Harmon 67.20%, Show-o2 68.89%, Tar-7B 67.91%), versus aligned VLMs/LLMs at 84.5-98.1% (DeepSeek-VL-Chat 84.48%, Qwen2.5-VL 92.63%, Qwen2.5 98.10%). A clear closed-open gap exists among full-task-coverage (7/7) models: Gemini-2.5-flash-image reaches Both-Safe=87.17% versus open-source Tar-7B at 67.91% and Bagel at 53.11%. The first-token-entropy probe shows a mechanistic correlate: Qwen2.5-7B has entropy=0.097, Top-1 prob=0.971, refusal margin=+7.544 on unsafe text prompts, while its unified derivative Tar-7B flattens to entropy=0.537, Top-1 prob=0.857, refusal margin=-6.409, and Bagel degrades further to entropy=2.880, refusal mass~1.78e-5, refusal margin=-11.918 -- since this pattern holds even on pure-text-only inputs, the authors argue unification itself (not just added multimodal perception difficulty) erodes the sharp refusal-token preference of the base LLM. A safety-utility trade-off is also documented: Chameleon achieves near-perfect safety (Both-Safe>0.98, 99.31% overall) purely via indiscriminate refusal (C-Reject=82.67%, Utility=0.245, the lowest usefulness score in the benchmark), whereas GPT-4o attains a better Pareto point (Both-Safe=94.61%, Utility>0.85) without pathological refusal. A modality-asymmetry finding: unified models show higher safety on Dual-Unsafe VQA (both modalities malicious) than Image-Unsafe VQA (only image malicious) -- e.g., JanusFlow scores 0.505 Both-Safe on Dual-Unsafe vs. 0.193 on Image-Unsafe -- indicating textual safety alignment transfers poorly to purely visual attack vectors.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Uni-SafeBench, a safety benchmark specifically designed for Unified Multimodal Large Models (UMLMs) -- models that integrate multimodal understanding and generation in a single architecture (e.g., Chameleon, Janus-Pro, JanusFlow, Emu3, Harmon, Show-o2, Bagel, Tar, GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-flash-image). Unlike prior safety benchmarks that evaluate understanding-only or generation-only tasks in isolation, Uni-SafeBench spans seven task types (Dual-Unsafe VQA, Image-Unsafe VQA, Text-Unsafe VQA, Composite-Unsafe VQA, Unsafe T2I Generation, Malicious Image Editing, Malicious Text Generation) organized under a taxonomy of six top-level risk categories (Toxic Content, Biased Opinion, Physical and Mental Harm, Unethical Activities, Privacy Leakage, Sensitive Topics) refined into 16 fine-grained subcategories. The dataset comprises 2,939 malicious queries, built partly by re-mapping/filtering existing sources (AdvBench, SIUO, VLSafe, HADES) into the new taxonomy and partly by newly constructing data (especially for malicious image editing, a task with no prior compatible benchmark) via an LLM-drafting + human-refinement pipeline. The paper also proposes Uni-Judger, an automated dual-perspective evaluator with a Malicious Intent Extractor (E) and a Safety Detector (F) that separately scores Contextual Safety (y_ctx in {Safe, Unsafe, Reject}, judged against the extracted malicious intent) and Intrinsic Safety (y_int in {Safe, Unsafe}, judged from the response alone, since 'Reject' cannot be identified without the original query). Four metrics are reported: C-Unsafe, C-Reject, I-Unsafe, and Both-Safe (the aggregate safety rate requiring both contextual and intrinsic safety). Using this framework, the paper's central empirical finding is a 'Unification-Induced Safety Degradation' effect: open-source unified models consistently show weaker safety than their own LLM/VLM backbones or specialized (non-unified) multimodal counterparts.
threat_model: Adversarial users submit malicious multimodal (text and/or image) requests to a UMLM across seven task formats spanning both understanding (VQA-style) and generation (text, text-to-image, image editing) modalities. Risks are grouped as Context-Dependent Risk (a response is benign in isolation but harmful when paired with the user's malicious intent, e.g., 'open the panel and disconnect the red wire' read against a high-voltage-equipment image) and Context-Independent Risk (the output itself is toxic regardless of intent, e.g., a graphic depiction of wounds even for an ostensibly neutral request). The paper does not address external adversarial perturbation/jailbreak attacks against model weights or training; its threat model is standard prompted-misuse/red-teaming of deployed multimodal models, evaluated via an LLM-as-judge pipeline rather than via novel attack construction.
limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge that their automatic evaluator, Uni-Judger, 'may not fully capture subtle or implicit harms (e.g., sarcasm, symbolic cues, or culturally specific content).' Intrinsic-Safety human agreement is markedly weaker for image-editing outputs (79.91%) than for other tasks, reflecting genuine ambiguity in judging edited images without prompt context, which the paper frames as a task-inherent limitation rather than a fixable evaluator flaw. The dataset's category distribution is deliberately long-tailed and not upsampled/balanced, so some category-level percentages in Table 4 are computed on small subgroups (e.g., N=60 for some subcategories), and the paper itself flags that certain per-category safety rates (e.g., Privacy Leak reaching 82.22% in T2I generation) may partly reflect a model's inability to render recognizable identities rather than genuine alignment. Several open-source unified models (marked with ‡, e.g., Chameleon, GPT-4o for some tasks) had image-generation APIs unavailable during evaluation, leaving generation-task cells blank for those models. The causal mechanism proposed for unification-induced degradation (first-token entropy/refusal-margin shift) is presented as a 'possible explanation' and 'suggestive,' not a rigorously isolated causal claim -- the authors use hedged language ('cautious conclusion') throughout rather than asserting a proven mechanism.

datasets_used: Uni-SafeBench (newly constructed, 2,939 malicious multimodal queries across 7 task types and 6 top-level / 16 fine-grained safety categories)
quantitative_results: Uni-Judger vs. human agreement: 94.42% weighted-average Contextual Safety accuracy, 93.71% Intrinsic Safety accuracy (Table 1)
Malicious Image Editing intrinsic-safety human agreement: 79.91% (vs. 93.01% contextual) -- lowest per-task agreement
Dual-metric non-redundancy: contextual-only errors 108/2939 (3.67%), intrinsic-only errors 130/2939 (4.42%), overlap 55/2939 (1.87%), both-correct 2646/2939 (90.03%)

Evaluator-robustness (large judge Qwen3-VL-235B vs. Qwen3-VL-8B): Contextual agreement 87.7% (kappa=0.693, macro-F1=0.845); Intrinsic agreement 91.6% (kappa=0.765, macro-F1=0.882); overall contextual unsafe-rate estimate shifts 23.3%->31.2%
Backbone-vs-unified Both-Safe on Malicious Text Generation: Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct 0.981 -> Bagel 0.514, Tar-7B 0.660; Janus-Pro 0.635 vs. DeepSeek-VL-Chat 0.978

First-token entropy/refusal-margin on 630 unsafe text prompts: Qwen2.5-7B entropy=0.097, Top-1=0.971, refusal mass=0.942, refusal margin=+7.544; Tar-7B entropy=0.537, Top-1=0.857, refusal mass=0.173, refusal margin=-6.409; Bagel entropy=2.880, Top-1=0.287, refusal mass=1.78e-5, refusal margin=-11.918

Overall Both-Safe (Table 5): Qwen2.5 98.10%, Chameleon 99.31%, GPT-4o 94.61%, Gemini-2.5 87.17%, Qwen2.5-VL 92.63%, DeepSeek-VL-Chat 84.48%, DeepSeek-VL-Base 61.59%, Janus-Pro 57.45%, JanusFlow 40.18%, Tar-1.5B 66.79%, Emu3 38.75%, Harmon 67.20%, Show-o2 68.89%, Bagel 53.11%, Tar-7B 67.91%, DeepSeek-LLM-Chat 91.75%

Utility scores (Table 2): Chameleon 0.245 (lowest, near-total refusal), GPT-4o 0.878-0.887, Gemini-2.5 0.854-0.865, Bagel 0.667 (text-gen), 0.375 (T2I), 69.41 (KRIS-Bench editing score), Tar-7B 33.5 (KRIS-Bench editing score)
Modality asymmetry: JanusFlow Both-Safe = 0.505 (Dual-Unsafe VQA, both modalities malicious) vs. 0.193 (Image-Unsafe VQA, image-only malicious)

Category-wise Bagel safety (Text-Unsafe VQA): overall weighted Both-Safe 38.55%, Unethical Activities 19.35%, Illegal Activities refusal rate 6.67% (N=60), Violation of Public Order refusal rate 15.00% (N=60); Unsafe T2I Gen overall 63.59%, Physical and Mental Harm 30.00%, Self-harm 20.00%, Dangerous Behavior 40.00%, Privacy Leak 82.22% (highest)
